name: FTC Click-to-Cancel Rule (vacated July 2025)
summary: Former federal rule requiring subscription cancellation to be as easy as signup. Vacated by the 8th Circuit Court of Appeals on July 8, 2025.
retirementReason: Vacated by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit on July 8, 2025, finding the rule 'arbitrary, capricious, and an abuse of discretion' under the Administrative Procedure Act.

evidence.claim: FTC finalized the Click-to-Cancel Rule (Negative Option Rule amendment) on October 16, 2024.
Compliance deadline was delayed from May 14 to July 14, 2025.
On July 8, 2025, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit vacated the rule, finding it 'arbitrary, capricious, and an abuse of discretion' under the APA.

businessScope.note: Rule is vacated; included in registry for historical audit and to clarify what federal-level rights DO NOT currently exist (state laws still apply where enacted).

body.officialName: Rule Concerning Recurring Subscriptions and Other Negative Option Programs (Click-to-Cancel Rule)
body.vacatedBy: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit
body.scopeSummary: The rule would have required sellers to: (a) obtain affirmative consent before charging for a negative-option subscription, (b) provide a cancellation mechanism as easy as signup, (c) prohibit misrepresentations in marketing of negative-option programs. Vacated by 8th Circuit on July 8, 2025 (E3).
body.userRightsPlainLanguage: This federal rule would have made it easier to cancel subscriptions in the US, but it was blocked by a federal court and is NOT currently in effect. State-level protections (like California SB-313) still apply to residents of those states. If you're in a state without a click-to-cancel law, you do not currently have federal-level protection.

enforcementSignals.description: CA, NY, VT, IL and other states have click-to-cancel laws at the state level.
